title: Kouign-amann
subtitle: Breton caramelised butter cake made from laminated yeasted dough
excerpt: A Breton speciality made by folding caster sugar and salt flakes into a simple yeasted dough with a large amount of butter. As it bakes, the sugar caramelises into a crisp, amber crust around a soft, pull-apart interior. Makes one round for eight.
sourceNotes: Kouign-amann was created in Douarnenez, Brittany, in 1860 by baker Yves-René Scordia, a date that places it firmly in the public domain. The Breton name means 'butter cake'. The original recipe used excess yeasted bread dough, butter, and sugar, making a caramelised round loaf. The technique of folding sugar and butter into bread dough to produce a laminated caramelised result is documented in French and Breton regional baking literature from the late nineteenth century onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Eat on the day. The caramel softens and the layers lose their definition by the next morning.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; the sugar should caramelise to a deep, glossy amber at the edges; check at 30 minutes and continue until the surface is firm and richly coloured

content.text: The sugar goes into the folds with the butter rather than into the dough; this is what produces the caramelised crust. Use a tin with a solid base, not a loose-bottomed one, as the melted butter and sugar will leak. The cake should be turned out upside-down onto the rack immediately after baking; if you let it cool in the tin the caramel will set and it will stick. Use oven gloves for this — the tin and the caramel are extremely hot.

items.prepNote: cold, bashed to a 15 cm square between two sheets of baking paper

content.text: Combine the {{strong-bread-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} (dough portion) in a bowl. Add the warm {{water}} and mix to a smooth dough. Knead for 5 minutes until elastic. Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave at room temperature for 30 minutes until slightly puffed.
Laminate with butter and sugar, 3 turns

Roll the dough on a lightly floured surface to a rectangle of about 20 × 40 cm. Place the cold {{unsalted-butter}} block in the centre of the dough and fold both sides over it to encase. Roll out to 20 × 50 cm. Scatter one-third of the {{caster-sugar}} and a pinch of the {{sea-salt-flakes}} over the surface. Fold the bottom third up and the top third down (a letter fold), wrap, and refrigerate for 60 to 70 minutes. Repeat twice more, using the remaining {{caster-sugar}} and {{sea-salt-flakes}} in the second and third folds, chilling between each.

Heavily butter a 20 cm solid-based round cake tin. Roll the dough out to a circle slightly larger than the tin. Transfer to the tin, tucking the edges under. Cover loosely and prove for 45 minutes until puffed. Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until the surface is a deep amber and the cake feels firm when pressed in the centre. Leave in the tin for 2 minutes only, then invert onto a wire rack placed over a sheet of baking paper to catch any drips. Serve warm.
